title: Configure High Availability and Workload Isolation

sections.heading: Considerations
sections.body:
- Atlas does not guarantee that host names remain consistent with
  respect to node types during topology changes.

  > **Example:** If you have a cluster named `foo123` containing an analytics
  > node `foo123-shard-00-03-a1b2c.mongodb.net:27017`, Atlas does
  > not guarantee that specific host name will continue to refer to an
  > analytics node after a topology change, such as
  > scaling a cluster to modify its
  > number of nodes or regions.

- In sharded clusters that use a [dedicated config server](config-server-types), Atlas distributes the three config server
  nodes based on the number of electable regions in the cluster. If the
  cluster has:

  - Only one electable region, Atlas deploys all three config
    nodes in that region.

  - Two electable regions, Atlas deploys two config nodes in the
    highest priority region and one config node in the second highest
    priority region.

  - Three or more electable regions, Atlas deploys one config node
    in each of the three highest priority regions.

- Having a large number of regions or having nodes spread across
  long distances may lead to long election times or replication lag.

- A cluster change that adds, removes, or modifies voting members will take longer, since
  Atlas adds, removes, or modifies [voting members](manual/core/replica-set-elections) one at a time on a [Atlas High Availability](high-availability).

- Clusters can span regions and cloud service providers. The total number
  of nodes in clusters spanning across regions has a specific constraint
  on a per-project basis.

  Atlas limits the total number of nodes in other regions in one
  project to a total of 40, **not** including:

  - Google Cloud regions communicating with each other

  - Free clusters

  - Flex clusters

  Sharded clusters include additional nodes. The electable nodes on
  the dedicated Config Server Replica Set (CSRS) count towards the
  total number of allowable nodes. Each sharded cluster has an
  additional electable node per region as part of the dedicated CSRS. To
  learn more, see [Replica Set Config Servers](manual/core/sharded-cluster-config-servers).

  The total number of nodes between any two regions must meet this
  constraint.

  > **Example:** If an Atlas project has nodes in clusters spread across three
  > regions:
  >
  > - 30 nodes in **Region A**
  >
  > - 10 nodes in **Region B**
  >
  > - 5 nodes in **Region C**
  >
  > You can only add 5 more nodes to **Region C** because:
  >
  > - If you exclude Region C, Region A + Region B = 40.
  >
  >
  > - If you exclude Region B, Region A + Region C = 35, \<= 40.
  >
  >
  > - If you exclude Region A, Region B + Region C = 15, \<= 40.
  >
  >
  > - Each combination of regions with the added 5 nodes still meets
  >   the per-project constraint:
  >
  >   - Region A + B = 40 
  >
  >   - Region A + C = 40 
  >
  >   - Region B + C = 20 

  You can't create a multi-region cluster in a project if it has one or
  more clusters spanning 40 or more nodes in other regions.

  Contact Atlas [support](manual/support) for questions
  or assistance with raising this limit.

- Atlas provides built-in custom write concerns for multi-region
  clusters. Use these write concerns to ensure your write operations
  propagate to a desired number of regions, thereby ensuring data
  consistency across your regions. To learn more, see
  [Built-In Custom Write Concerns](built-in-custom-wc).

- The number of availability zones, zones, or fault domains in a region has no effect on the number of MongoDB
  nodes Atlas can deploy. MongoDB Atlas clusters are always made of
  replica sets with a minimum of three MongoDB nodes.

- If you use the [standard connection string format](manual/reference/connection-string)
  rather than the DNS (Domain Name System) seedlist format, removing an entire region from an
  existing cross-region cluster may result in a new connection string.

  To verify the correct connection string after deploying the changes:

  # Step 1: Click .

- If you plan on creating one or more VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) peering connections on
  your first `M10+` dedicated paid cluster for the selected region
  or regions, first review the documentation on
  VPC Peering Connections.

- Atlas may use different cloud provider hardware for `M10` and `M20`
  clusters in specific [Google Cloud Platform (GCP)](google-gcp) regions. As a result,
  certain [Google Cloud Platform (GCP)](google-gcp) region combinations may not be supported by
  multi-region `M10` and `M20` clusters.

sections.heading: Electable Nodes for High Availability
sections.body:
If you add regions with electable nodes, you:

- increase data availability

- reduce the impact of data center outages

You can deploy nodes to multiple regions within the same cloud provider
or across multiple cloud providers.

Atlas sets the node in the first row of the Electable
nodes table as the Highest Priority region. Atlas
prioritizes nodes in this region for [primary](manual/reference/glossary) eligibility. Other
nodes rank in the order that they appear. For more information, see
[Member Priority](manual/core/replica-set-elections).

Each electable node can:

- Participate in replica set elections

- Become the [primary](manual/reference/glossary) while the majority of nodes in the
  replica set remain available

> **Important:** 
>
> When Global Writes are enabled, Global Clusters do not support dedicated search nodes.

sections.heading: Add Electable Nodes
sections.body:
You can add electable nodes in one cloud provider and region from the
**Electable nodes for high availability** section.

To add an electable node:

- Click **Add a provider/region**.

- Select the cloud provider from the **Provider** dropdown.

- Select the region from the **Region** dropdown.

  When you change the **Provider** option, the
  **Region** changes to a blank option. If you don't select a
  region, Atlas displays an error when you click
  **Create Cluster**.

- Specify the desired number of **Nodes** for the provider and
  region.

  The total number of electable nodes across all providers and regions
  in the cluster must equal **3**, **5**, or **7**.

Atlas considers regions marked with a star as
recommended. These regions provide higher availability compared to
other regions.

To learn more, see:

- [availability zones](amazon-aws-availability-zones)

- [GCP zones](google-gcp-availability-zones)

- [availability zones](microsoft-azure-availability-zones)

sections.heading: Remove Electable Nodes
sections.body:
To remove a node from a region, click the trash-alt icon to the
right side of that region. You cannot remove a node in the
**Highest Priority** region.

To learn more, see [Multi-Region Cluster Backups](multi-region-cloud-backup).

sections.heading: Change Electable Nodes to Read-Only Nodes
sections.body:
You can change an electable node to a [read-only nodes](deploy-read-only-replicas) by adding a read-only node and removing an
electable node at the same time. To learn more, see [Change Workload Purpose of Nodes](change-workload-purpose).

sections.heading: Improve the Availability of a Cluster
sections.body:
To improve the redundancy and availability of a cluster, increase the
number of electable nodes in that region. Every Atlas cluster has a
**Highest Priority** region. If your cluster spans multiple
regions, you can select which cloud provider region should be the
**Highest Priority**.

To prevent loss of availability and performance, consider the following
scenarios:

| Point of Failure | How to Prevent this Point of Failure                                                                              |
| ---------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cloud Provider   | Minimum of one node set in all three cloud providers. More than one node per region.                              |
| Region           | Minimum of one node set in three or more different regions. More than one node per region.                        |
| Node             | Three or more electable nodes in a Recommended region orThree or more electable nodes across two or more regions. |

sections.heading: Change the Highest Priority Provider or Region
sections.body:
If you change the **Highest Priority** provider or region in an
active multi-region cluster, Atlas selects a new [primary](manual/reference/glossary)
node in the provider and region that you specify (assuming that the
number of nodes in each provider and region remains the same and nothing
else is modified).

If Cloud Backup is enabled on your cluster when you change the
primary region, Atlas takes an on-demand snapshot and stores it in
the new primary region without waiting for the next scheduled snapshot.
If the new primary region was previously a secondary backup region for
[snapshot copy distribution](snapshot-distribution), Atlas
automatically updates your [cluster-level backup policy](configure-backup-policy) to distribute snapshot copies from the new
primary region to the original primary region and other secondary
regions.

> **Example:** If you have an active 5-node cluster with the following
> configuration:
>
> | Nodes | Provider                  | Region    | Priority |
> | ----- | ------------------------- | --------- | -------- |
> | 3     | AWS (Amazon Web Services) | us-east-1 | Highest  |
> | 2     | Google Cloud              | us-west3  |          |
>
> To make the Google Cloud **us-west3** nodes the **Highest
> Priority**, drag its row to the top of your cluster's
> **Electable nodes** list. After this change, Atlas elects a
> new **PRIMARY** in **us-west3**. Atlas doesn't start an initial
> sync or re-provision hosts when changing this configuration.

> **Important:** Certain circumstances may delay an election of a new
> [primary](manual/reference/glossary).
>
> For example, a sharded cluster with heavy workloads on its [primary](manual/reference/glossary)
> shard may delay the election. This results in not having all
> primary nodes in the same region temporarily.
>
> To minimize these risks, avoid modifying your primary region during
> periods of heavy workload.

sections.heading: Read-Only Nodes for Optimal Local Reads
sections.body:
Use read-only nodes to optimize local reads in the nodes' respective
service areas.

sections.heading: Add Read-Only Nodes
sections.body:
You can add read-only nodes from the
**Read-Only Nodes for Optimal Local Reads** section.

To add a read-only node in one cloud provider and region:

- Click **Add a provider/region**.

- Select the cloud provider from the **Provider** dropdown.

- Select the region from the **Region** dropdown.

  When you change the **Provider** option, the
  **Region** changes to a blank option. If you don't select a
  region, Atlas displays an error when you click
  **Create Cluster**.

- Specify the desired number of **Nodes** for the provider and
  region.

Atlas considers regions marked with a star as
recommended. These regions provide higher availability compared to
other regions.

Read-only nodes don't provide high availability because they don't
participate in [elections](manual/reference/glossary). They can't become the
[primary](manual/reference/glossary) for their cluster. To direct queries to read-only nodes,
use [pre-defined replica set tags](replica-set-tags).

sections.heading: Remove Read-Only Nodes
sections.body:
To remove all read-only nodes in one cloud provider and region, click
the trash-alt icon to the right of that cloud provider and
region.

sections.heading: Change Workload Purpose of Nodes
sections.body:
You can change a node's workload purpose by adding and removing nodes at the
same time.

> **Note:** You must add and remove the node within the same configuration change
> to reuse a node. If you remove the node, save the change, then add the node,
> Atlas provisions a new node instead.

For example, to change a read-only node to an [electable node](deploy-across-multiple-cloud-providers):

- [add electable nodes](add-electable-node).

- [Remove a read-only node](remove-read-only-node).

- Click **Review Changes**.

- Click **Apply Changes**.

sections.heading: Analytics Nodes for Workload Isolation
sections.body:
Use [analytics nodes](analytics-nodes-overview) to isolate
queries which you do not wish to contend with your operational
workload. Analytics nodes help handle data analysis operations, such as
reporting queries from BI Connector for Atlas. To direct queries to analytics nodes,
use [pre-defined replica set tags](replica-set-tags).

Click Add a region to select a region in which to
deploy analytics nodes. Specify the desired number of
Nodes for the region.

> **Note:** The [readPreference](manual/reference/connection-string) and
> [readPreferenceTags](manual/reference/connection-string) connection
> string options are unavailable for the [mongo](manual/mongo) shell. To learn more, see [cursor.readPref()](manual/reference/method/cursor.readPref)
> and [Mongo.setReadPref()](manual/reference/method/Mongo.setReadPref) instead.

sections.heading: Add Analytics Nodes
sections.body:
You can add analytics nodes from the
**Analytics nodes for workload isolation** section.

To add analytics nodes in one cloud provider and region:

- Click **Add a provider/region**.

- Select the cloud provider from the **Provider** dropdown.

- Select the region from the **Region** dropdown.

  When you change the **Provider** option, the
  **Region** changes to a blank option. If you don't select a
  region, Atlas displays an error when you click
  **Create Cluster**.

- Specify the desired number of **Nodes** for the provider and
  region.

Atlas considers regions marked with a star as
recommended. These regions provide higher availability compared to
other regions.

Analytics nodes don't provide high availability because they don't
participate in [elections](manual/reference/glossary). They can't become the
[primary](manual/reference/glossary) for their cluster.

sections.heading: Select a Cluster Tier for Your Analytics Nodes
sections.body:
Your workloads can vary greatly between analytics and operational nodes.
To help manage this issue, for `M10+` clusters, you can
[Select Cluster Tier](create-cluster-instance)
appropriately sized for your analytics workload. You can select a
cluster tier for your analytics nodes that's larger or smaller than
the cluster tier selected for your electable and read-only nodes
(operational nodes). This functionality helps to ensure that you get
the performance required for your transactional and analytical queries
without over or under provisioning your entire cluster for your
analytical workload.

The following considerations apply to the Analytics Tier
tab and analytic nodes:

> **Important:** If you select a cluster tier on the Analytics Tier
> tab significantly below the cluster tier selected on the
> Base Tier tab, [replication lag](manual/tutorial/troubleshoot-replica-sets)
> might result. The analytics node might fall off the [oplog](manual/reference/glossary)
> altogether.

- If you select a General cluster tier on the
  Analytics Tier tab and a Low-CPU cluster
  tier on the Base Tier tab, disk auto-scaling isn't
  supported for the cluster. Disk auto-scaling also isn't
  supported if you select a General cluster tier on the
  Base Tier tab and a Low-CPU cluster
  tier on the Analytics Tier tab.

- Disk size and IOPS must remain the same across all node
  types.

- Storage size must match between the Base Tier tab and
  Analytics Tier tab. You can set the storage size on the
  Base Tier tab.

- If you want to select the Local NVME SSD class on the
  Base Tier tab, the Analytics Tier tab
  must have the same tier level selected.

- If a cluster tier appears grayed out, the cluster tier isn't
  compatible with the disk size of the cluster or the
  Local NVME SSD class.

- A cluster tier selected on the Analytics Tier tab is
  priced the same as a cluster tier selected on the
  Base Tier tab. However, when an
  Analytics Tier is higher or lower than the
  Base Tier, the price adjusts accordingly on a
  prorated per-node basis. The pricing appears in the Atlas UI when
  you create or edit a cluster. To learn more, see,
  [Billing Cost Explorer](atlas-billing).

After you [Analytics nodes](add-analytics-node), you can
[Select Cluster Tier](create-cluster-instance)
appropriately sized for your analytics workload.

- In the **Cluster Tier** section, click the
  **Analytics Tier** tab.

- Select the **Cluster Tier**.

sections.heading: Remove Analytics Nodes
sections.body:
To remove all analytics nodes in one cloud provider and region, click
the trash-alt icon to the right of that cloud provider and
region.

sections.heading: Search Nodes for Workload Isolation
sections.body:
You can set the nodes in your cluster to run only the MongoDB Search
`mongot` process. When you run the MongoDB Search `mongot` process
separately, you improve the availability and workload balancing of the
`mongot` process. To learn more, see [Search Nodes](what-is-search-node).

sections.heading: Considerations
sections.body:
On `M10` or higher Atlas clusters running MongoDB v6.0 and
higher, you can configure Search Nodes separately from the database
nodes. Review the following before deploying MongoDB Search nodes separately.

sections.heading: Cluster Tier
sections.body:
You can deploy Search Nodes for dedicated (`M10` or higher)
clusters only. You can't add Search Nodes on free (`M0`) tier clusters
or Flex clusters. You can use the Atlas UI and the Atlas Administration API
to provision Search Nodes for new and existing clusters on AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud, or Azure (Microsoft Azure).

sections.heading: Cloud Provider
sections.body:
You can host the Search Nodes on any cloud provider.
You can't deploy Search Nodes separately
for [Manage Global Clusters](global-clusters).

sections.heading: Cloud Provider Regions
sections.body:
Atlas deploys the Search Nodes in the same AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud, or Azure (Microsoft Azure)
regions as your electable, read-only, and analytics nodes.

Atlas supports deploying Search Nodes separately for workload
isolation in only specific AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud, and Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions. The
following Atlas UI behavior applies:

- If you select any of the following AWS (Amazon Web Services) or Azure (Microsoft Azure) regions for your
  cluster nodes first, Atlas disables the Search
  nodes for workload isolation toggle.

- If you enable Search nodes for workload isolation
  first, Atlas disables the following in the dropdown for regions
  under Electable nodes for high availability.

Atlas doesn't support the following AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud, and Azure (Microsoft Azure)
regions for search nodes.

AWS

Google Cloud

Azure

To deploy Search Nodes separately, you must select a supported AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud,
or Azure (Microsoft Azure) region from the **Electable nodes for high availability**
dropdown. To learn more about the supported regions, see [Cloud Providers and Regions](create-cluster-cloud-provider-region).
Atlas automatically uses the same region for read-only and analytics
nodes on your cluster. After deployment, you can't change the
cloud provider or cloud provider region for your Atlas cluster.

sections.heading: Multi-Region and Multi-Cloud Clusters
sections.body:
Atlas supports deploying Search Nodes across multiple
regions and cloud providers. When deploying Search Nodes
for multi-region or multi-cloud clusters, consider the following:

- Atlas deploys the same number of Search Nodes
  in each region.

- All nodes across regions have the same [Search Tier](select-tiers-for-search-nodes).

- If you use the [Atlas Administration API](atlas-admin-api)
  to add a new region to existing Search Nodes, Atlas deploys
  the same number of Search Nodes in the new region. However, if the
  new region does not support the current search tier, the
  request fails.

sections.heading: Sharded Clusters with Search Nodes
sections.body:
Atlas requires a minimum of two Search Nodes for every region in a
Atlas cluster. This requirement applies to all cluster types,
including sharded clusters.

When deploying sharded clusters that rely heavily on MongoDB Search or
MongoDB Vector Search, understanding the Search Node topology requirements is crucial
for ensuring high availability and optimal performance. The following
topology requirements apply specifically to sharded clusters with Search
Nodes:

- **Multi-Region Deployments**: If your sharded cluster spans multiple
  regions, Atlas automatically deploys the specified number of Search
  Nodes in each region that contains database nodes.

- **Minimum Number of Nodes**: Each region with database nodes must have at
  least two Search Nodes to maintain availability during maintenance
  operations or node failures.

sections.heading: Search Tier
sections.body:
You can select a search tier for your Search Nodes in the
Search Tier tab.

By default, Atlas deploys Search Nodes on
`S20`. You can select a higher tier for faster queries and more
complex aggregations, or a lower tier for smaller workloads. If you
want to downsize to a lower tier, the search tier must accommodate
any existing search indexes, along with additional disk space for
bumper files and logs.

For Search Nodes deployed on AWS (Amazon Web Services), Atlas provides different search
tiers in different regions. If the search tier that you selected is not
available for your region, Atlas automatically deploys the search
nodes in the next higher tier that is available in that region.
To learn more, see [AWS Search Tiers](amazon-aws-search-tiers).

To learn more about search tiers for Search Nodes deployed on Google Cloud
or Azure (Microsoft Azure), see:

- [GCP Search Tiers](google-gcp-search-tiers)

- [Azure Search Tiers](microsoft-azure-search-tiers)

sections.heading: Enable Encryption at Rest
sections.body:
By default, MongoDB and [search processes](about-mongot) run on the same nodes.
With this architecture, customer-managed encryption applies to your database data,
but it does not apply to search indexes.

When you enable [Search Nodes](what-is-search-node), search processes run on separate nodes. This allows
you to enable [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption),
so you can encrypt both database data and search indexes with the same
customer-managed keys for comprehensive encryption coverage.

> **Note:** Database nodes and Search Nodes use different encryption methods
> with the same customer-managed keys. Database nodes use the
> [WiredTiger Encrypted Storage Engine](manual/core/security-encryption-at-rest),
> while Search Nodes use encryption at the disk level.

To learn more, see
[Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption).

> **Important:** This feature is available across KMS (Key Management Service) providers, but the
> Search Nodes must be on AWS (Amazon Web Services).

sections.heading: Add Search Nodes
sections.body:
Atlas UI

Atlas CLI

To create a search node for a cluster using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas clusters search nodes create [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas clusters search nodes create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-clusters-search-nodes-create).

Atlas Administration API

When you add Search Nodes to a cluster that doesn't have any search
nodes, the existing `mongot` processes running alongside `mongod`
tail writes to the database that happen after MongoDB Search completed the
initial sync and the `mongot` processes on the new dedicated search
nodes perform an initial sync on the required collections. This
results in dual reads during the migration process.

sections.heading: Add Search Nodes
sections.body:
To configure separate Search Nodes on your Atlas cluster,
do the following:

- Toggle **Multi-Cloud, Multi-Region & Workload
  Isolation (M10+ clusters)** to **On**.

- Toggle **Search nodes for workload isolation** to
  **On**.

- Specify the number of nodes to deploy. You can specify between
  a minimum of 2 and a maximum of 32 nodes.

  After deployment, you can modify the cluster to add and remove
  any additional Search Nodes.

  > **Note:** For multi-region clusters, Atlas deploys
  > the specified number of nodes in each region.
  > To learn more, see [Multi-Region and Multi-Cloud Clusters](multi-region-search-nodes).

- Expand **Cluster Tier** to select a tier for your search
  nodes in the **Search Tiers** tab.

  To learn more about the different tiers for your Search Nodes, see
  [Search Tier](select-tiers-for-search-nodes).

sections.heading: Add Search Nodes
sections.body:
> **Note:** You can add Search Nodes to existing Atlas cluster only
> if you currently don't have any and have never created MongoDB Search
> indexes on your cluster.

To configure separate Search Nodes on your Atlas cluster,
send a `POST` request to the [MongoDB Search](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-atlas-search) resource `/deployment` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupclustersearchdeployment). You must specify the number of
nodes and the [Search Tier](select-tiers-for-search-nodes). You can deploy between a minimum
of 2 and a maximum of 32 nodes. To learn more, see
[Create Search Nodes](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupclustersearchdeployment).

sections.heading: Remove Search Nodes
sections.body:
Atlas UI

Remove Some

To remove some Search Nodes, adjust the **Number of
Search Nodes** setting under **Search nodes for workload
isolation**. You can deploy between 2 and 32 Search Nodes on your
cluster.

Remove All

> **Important:** If you remove all Search Nodes, you lose
> Search Node encryption with [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption). Your data
> will still use Atlas's default encryption, but
> your search workloads and indexes will no longer be
> encrypted with your customer-managed keys.

To remove all Search Nodes on your Atlas cluster,
do the following:

- Toggle **Search nodes for workload isolation** to
  **Off**.

  You can now select any region from the available cloud
  providers.

- Click **Remove** to confirm in the **Are
  you sure you want to remove Search Nodes?** modal.

- Click **Review Changes**.

- Click the checkbox to confirm that you understand and agree to
  the considerations for deleting a cluster with Search Nodes.

  There will be a brief interruption in processing your search
  query results.

- Click **Apply Changes**.

Atlas CLI

To delete a search node for a cluster using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas clusters search nodes delete [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas clusters search nodes delete](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-clusters-search-nodes-delete).

Atlas Administration API

Remove Some

To add or remove Search Nodes or modify search tier on your
Atlas cluster, send a `PATCH` request to the
[MongoDB Search](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-atlas-search) resource
`/deployment` [Update Search Nodes](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updateatlassearchdeployment) endpoint.

You can specify the following:

- The number of nodes to remove. If omitted, Atlas doesn't
  change the current number of deployed nodes.

- The [Search Tier](select-tiers-for-search-nodes) to use
  to switch to a different search tier. If omitted, Atlas
  doesn't modify the current search tier for the deployed nodes.

To learn more, see [Update Search Nodes](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updateatlassearchdeployment).

Remove All

> **Important:** If you remove all Search Nodes, you lose
> Search Node encryption with [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption). Your data
> will still use Atlas's default encryption, but
> your search workloads and indexes will no longer be
> encrypted with your customer-managed keys.

To remove all Search Nodes on your Atlas cluster,
send a `DELETE` request to the [MongoDB Search](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-atlas-search) resource `/deployment` [endpoint](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-deleteatlassearchdeployment). To learn more, see
[Delete Search Nodes](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-deleteatlassearchdeployment).

If you delete all existing Search Nodes on your cluster, there will
be an interruption in processing your search query results while
Atlas migrates from `mongot` processes running separately on
dedicated Search Nodes to `mongot` processes running alongside
`mongod`. You can't run queries while indexes are building on the
node that hosts both the `mongot` and `mongod` processes, and you
will experience query downtime.

sections.heading: Remove Search Nodes
sections.body:
> **Important:** If you remove all Search Nodes, you lose
> Search Node encryption with [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption). Your data
> will still use Atlas's default encryption, but
> your search workloads and indexes will no longer be
> encrypted with your customer-managed keys.

sections.heading: Connections to Multi-Cloud Deployments
sections.body:
If you're connecting to replica sets hosted in a multi-cloud deployment
through a [private connection](conn-string-options), you can access
only the nodes in the same cloud provider and the same region that you're connecting
from. This cloud provider might not have the [primary](manual/reference/glossary) node in its region.
When this happens, you must specify the [secondary read preference](manual/core/read-preference)
mode in the connection string to access the deployment. If you need access
to all nodes for your multi-cloud deployment from your current provider through
a private connection, you must:

- Configure a VPN in the current provider to each of the remaining
  providers.

- Configure a [private endpoint connections](private-endpoint) to Atlas
  for each of the remaining providers.

As an alternative, we suggest creating sharded clusters for multi-cloud deployments accessed
through a [private endpoint connections](private-endpoint). When you deploy a sharded
cluster, whether single shard or multiple shards, you can connect to the cluster
through the local private endpoints and perform read and write operations. You are not required
to specify the secondary read preference mode, even if the cluster primary node(s) are located
in a different region. The `mongos` in the cloud provider region you connect to will route the
request to the primary node in a different region if needed. To learn more, see
[Deploy a Sharded Cluster](create-cluster-sharding).
